north_star_verdict.headline: A genuinely-interested Shortlisted fit deal that has quietly stalled: Christina Behm has pushed the close five times into an opaque internal-approvals loop, the budget owner has not been in a room since the 13 Feb demo, and with a negative AI read and no quantified value case there is nothing concrete for approvals to price.
north_star_verdict.math: Today 6 Jul 2026. Close 28 Jul = 22 days out. Shortlisted since 30 Mar 2026; last buyer-facing deliverable was the 13 Feb scenario demo (143 days ago). Close date pushed five times (31 Mar to 29 Apr to 20 May to 16 Jun to 29 Jun to 28 Jul). Buyer in internal approvals since 26 May; Christina asked to reconnect only the third week of July, on/after the close date. Pricing acceptance, budget validation, on-site, InfoSec, MSA/SOW and signature all remain: realistically 8-12 weeks, pushing a real close to mid-to-late Sep 2026.
north_star_verdict.recommended_forecast: Pipeline / push close to mid-late Sep 2026
north_star_verdict.engagement_read: Buyer contact is polite but thinning: Christina's 22 May and 12 Jun emails were both 'we're busy, we'll update you' with no substance, and every close-date move and follow-up since April has been rep-initiated. A key action (internal approval) is genuinely stalled and the cadence is decaying, which is why this is Slowing rather than a healthy-but-late Close Date Risk.
evidence: Next Step 29 Jun 2026: 'Christina follow up; closing FY end of June. asked to connect 3rd week of July' (SF Next_Step__c)
Email 12 Jun 2026, Christina Behm: 'June is fiscal year end... we should have an update for you' (SF Task)
Email 22 May 2026, Christina Behm: 'Sorry for the non-response. We are still working through a few things' (SF Task)
CloseDate slipped five times 31 Mar to 28 Jul 2026 (SF OpportunityFieldHistory)
Tim Drost, scenario demo 13 Feb 2026: 'we see a lot of good things... some genuine interest' but 'we don't wanna drag and drag'

metrics.narrative: The pain is vivid and owner-attributed but has never been quantified after seven months. On the 16 Jan demo Grace recapped Pep's own numbers: onboarding time ranging 'anywhere from 7 days to 9 months' across roughly 150-200 suppliers, manual document chasing, and manual tax/banking verification by accounting. Yet Christina Behm said on the 17 Dec discovery 'I don't have a timeline or a budget,' and no dollar cost of manual exception-tracking, COI lapses, or onboarding delay has been put on paper. Building that quantified cost-of-inaction case is the single lever that gives internal approvals something to approve.

economic_buyer.narrative: Tim Drost is the budget-controlling voice and drove the entire commercial conversation on the 13 Feb scenario call: 'how do you guys bundle this thing up to sell it?', 'how do we buy this from you', probing whether the iSupplier/iContract/iRisk suite is a one-time buy and what falls inside the packaged price. He is Christina's manager (Next Step 17 Dec 2025: 'Tim (manager) joined today'). So the economic buyer IS mapped and was engaged as recently as 13 Feb, but final budget sign-off now sits in an opaque 'internal approvals' process (SF Next_Step__c 26 May) that Tim has not visibly driven since Feb. The move is to broker a Tim conversation through Christina to validate the spend, not to leave EB as an unknown.

decision_criteria.narrative: The criteria are explicit and were validated live. Pep pre-supplied four exact onboarding-exception scenarios (E&O insurance threshold, client references under WPP expectation, limitation-of-liability cap, privacy/security documentation) and Srishti walked each one click-by-click on 13 Feb. Buyer-stated must-haves surfaced as questions: white-labelling ('you can white label the site, correct?'), conditional questioning, phased/multi-step approval workflows, delegation/impersonation for out-of-office approvers, exception reporting ('a report of all suppliers with insurance exceptions'), and SSO/embedded login from Pep Connect (Tim: 'I don't wanna create multiple logins'). Fit was strong: Tim concluded 'you're in the right area of the solution... not missing anything super vital.'
sources: Avoma scenario 13 Feb 2026 (Tim Drost, Jillian Lushman)

decision_process.narrative: The evaluation process was well mapped through Feb 2026 (intro 21 Nov, discovery 17 Dec, demo 16 Jan, scenario session 13 Feb), then went internal. Tim framed the buyer's own next step on 13 Feb: 'we reconnect, recalibrate our questions... and then either come back with a second agenda or swing down and see them,' with a Cincinnati on-site floated. Since then the decision has been an opaque internal-approvals cycle owned by Christina with no buyer-facing milestone. The approval chain, pricing acceptance, on-site, InfoSec and contracting sequence are undefined with the buyer; a written mutual close plan is the missing piece.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics are effectively unknown. An NDA was signed early (Next Step 10 Dec 2025) and Grace scoped implementation at 10-20 weeks with hypercare on 13 Feb, but there is no visibility into MSA/SOW, legal or redline path, procurement channel, or an InfoSec/security-review timeline, and as a net-new deal there is no contract-expiration forcing function. Surfacing the buyer's own legal, procurement and InfoSec sequence (who signs, what security review is required, what paper they use) is what would make any close date real.

identify_pain.narrative: The operational pain is specific and rising. Grace recapped it on 16 Jan: supplier onboarding is largely manual with heavy document chasing, onboarding time ranging 7 days to 9 months, no centralized tracking of contract red-lines/liability-cap deviations, and manual tracking of expirations and renewals. Exceptions were the recurring theme ('one of the words I kept hearing a lot'). The pain owners are Christina Behm (sourcing operations) and Jillian Lushman (sourcing lead), who today hold exception knowledge manually. The risk of inaction is real: expired COIs and untracked liability deviations create compliance and audit exposure.

champion.narrative: Christina Behm (Sourcing Operations Manager, Decision Maker on the contact roles) is a genuine working champion: she sent the NDA, curated the four exception scenarios for the demo, and is running the internal approval herself. But she is mid-level, single-threaded, and now in a holding pattern where her last two touches were 'we'll update you' non-answers (22 May, 12 Jun). Jillian Lushman (Sourcing Lead, evaluator) is engaged and supportive but low power. The higher-power sponsor Tim Drost was active on commercials 13 Feb but has not driven the deal since. 'Developing' because Christina advocates but has no quantified value case to carry to power and no second thread has been built.

competition.narrative: The primary alternative is the internal build (extend Pep Connect), kept explicitly alive by Tim Drost on 17 Dec 2025 ('we looked at building this... it still could happen') and backed by Pep's in-house developers. On 13 Feb Tim leaned toward buying ('how do we buy this from you... one-time buy of the suite'), which softens but does not retire the build. The more acute current threat is do-nothing inertia: 'it's not urgent, I don't have a timeline or a budget,' evidenced by five close slips and a three-month approvals stall. 'Multiple vendors' were referenced 17 Dec but never named. Our wedge (speed-to-value, freeing engineers for client work, native governance/AI depth) is real but not yet in a written buy-vs-build case for the budget owner.

deal_movement.summary: No buyer-facing progress since the 13 Feb scenario demo. Every movement since is rep-side chasing (VMs, emails, on-site nudges) or administrative close-date pushes; the buyer's own contact has thinned to two brief 'we'll update you' emails. The deal is held, not advancing.

items.change: Close date pushed a fifth time, 29 Jun to 28 Jul 2026 (still on/before the buyer's own 'third week of July' reconnect)
Christina emailed 'June is fiscal year end... we should have an update for you' (last genuine buyer touch)
Christina emailed 'still working through a few things', pushing the timeline
Stage advanced Formal Evaluation to Shortlisted; forecast moved Best Case to Upside Key Deal
89-minute scenario session validated the four onboarding-exception workflows; Tim drove the commercial/packaging conversation

competitive_position.summary: Recency-weighted, the strongest CURRENT threat is do-nothing inertia, not a rival vendor: the buyer has no budget or timeline ('it's not urgent'), has pushed the close five times, and has sat in opaque internal approvals since 26 May. The strongest structural alternative remains the internal build (extend Pep Connect), kept alive by Tim Drost 17 Dec 2025 ('it still could happen') though he leaned to buying on 13 Feb. Unnamed 'other vendors' were referenced once (17 Dec) but never surfaced across four calls, so they are a low current threat. We win by pricing the cost of inaction and building a quantified buy-vs-build case that beats internal-build speed and Pep's engineering opportunity cost.

competitors.name: Do-nothing / status quo (keep Pep Connect as-is)
competitors.quote: it's not urgent. I don't have a timeline or a budget
competitors.source: Avoma intro 21 Nov 2025 (Christina Behm); reinforced by five close slips and the 26 May-12 Jun approvals stall
competitors.how_we_win: Quantify the cost of inaction (COI/insurance lapse exposure, exceptions living in staff heads, 7-day-to-9-month onboarding drag) and tie it to a fiscal-year forcing event so continuing manual work carries a visible price.

competitors.name: Internal build (extend Pep Connect system)
competitors.quote: We looked at building this, and it still could happen. We wanna focus on our clients and building stuff for our clients and not on this.
competitors.source: Avoma discovery 17 Dec 2025 (Tim Drost); softened but not retracted on 13 Feb ('how do we buy this... one-time buy of the suite')
competitors.how_we_win: Speed-to-value (10-20 week implementation vs a 12-18 month build), keep Pep's developers on client-facing products not internal plumbing, and native governance plus Merlin contract/risk intelligence Pep cannot build quickly; make it a written buy-vs-build case for the budget owner.

competitors.name: Other third-party vendors (unnamed)
competitors.quote: We truly are engaging a number of folks and gonna make a decision here on a path forward
competitors.source: Avoma discovery 17 Dec 2025 (Tim Drost); SF Next_Step__c 17 Dec 2025 'evaluating multiple vendors'
competitors.how_we_win: No rival named across four calls and seven months, so lock the validated iSupplier/iContract/iRisk fit into a written differentiated proposal before any competitor gets a name.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Strong workflow fit on the exact exception scenarios Pep runs; the open exposures are adoption/integration (SSO/embedded login, IT-questionnaire integration) and the buyer's disinterest in the AI/Merlin layer.

items.criterion: Configurable exception-driven onboarding workflows (insurance, references, liability, security)
items.quote: we configured specific onboarding tabs that mirror the exact scenarios that you asked us to demonstrate today
You can white label the site, correct? ... can you have conditional questioning

items.criterion: Exception reporting and risk visibility per supplier
items.quote: would we be able to pull a report... here is all the suppliers that have insurance exceptions

items.criterion: SSO / embedded single-login from Pep Connect for suppliers
items.quote: what I don't wanna create is multiple logins for multiple users... a little embedded link inside our system

items.criterion: Integration with standard purchased IT security questionnaires (e.g. VSA)
items.quote: is it all customized questionnaires, or do you have partnerships with any of those IT questionnaires... let me confirm with my integration team
no AI, Merlin, Agentic, Intake or GenAI reference across the 89-minute demo

items.deliverable: Christina to share the internal-approvals decision and an updated timeline she committed to on 12 Jun

items.quote: I don't have a timeline or a budget
no contracting mechanics discussed with the buyer

best_practice_check.summary: Three levers matter most now: break the single-thread to the budget owner, arm the champion with a quantified value case, and re-create a forcing event after five close slips.
flags: Multi-thread beyond Christina to budget owner Tim Drost and to an InfoSec contact before the third-week-July reconnect; the deal is a single point of failure on one mid-level champion.
Convert the validated 13 Feb fit into a written buy-vs-build business case that prices manual-exception and onboarding-delay cost; internal approvals have nothing quantified to approve.
Re-establish a buyer-owned forcing event (the floated Cincinnati on-site) since the close has slipped five times with no milestone the buyer owns.

champion_strength.summary: Christina Behm is a genuine working champion running the internal approval herself, but she is mid-level, single-threaded, and now in a holding pattern with nothing quantified to carry to power; no secondary champion has been built and the higher-power sponsor Tim Drost has not re-engaged since 13 Feb.
champion_strength.source: All four calls 21 Nov 2025 - 13 Feb 2026; SF OpportunityContactRole; SF Tasks 22 May / 12 Jun 2026

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Merlin and Merlin Intake sit in the product scope, but the buyer voiced zero AI interest across the 89-minute 13 Feb demo and locked direction to iContract/iSupplier/iRisk. This is buyer disinterest, not weak positioning; do not force the agentic narrative, sell the governance/workflow value and let the InstaReview clause-risk and iRisk scoring speak for themselves.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AIS 2.0 / Negative is accurate and corroborated by the calls: across the 89-minute 13 Feb demo no Pep stakeholder referenced AI, Merlin, Agentic, Intake or GenAI, and the confirmed scope dropped Merlin/Intake in favour of three core modules. The field is not under-rated.

scope_change.from: iSupplier, iContract, iRisk plus Merlin and Merlin Intake (AI-inclusive suite)
scope_change.to: iContract, iSupplier, iRisk Lite only (AI/Merlin dropped)
scope_change.detail: The 16 Jan demo introduced Merlin Intake and AI tooling, but across the 89-minute 13 Feb scenario session Pep voiced zero AI/Merlin interest and confirmed direction as the three core modules with iRisk Lite. A defensive narrowing consistent with a cost-conscious, phased buy and the negative AIS.

confidence_signals.summary: Engagement is cooling: rep-side follow-ups every week or two since April, but buyer replies have thinned to brief non-substantive 'we'll update you' notes and the close has slipped five times.
items: Last genuine buyer touch was an email on 12 Jun 2026; no call since 13 Feb 2026
Two consecutive buyer emails (22 May, 12 Jun) were delays with no new information
Every close-date change and re-engagement attempt since April was rep-initiated

customer_preference.evidence: Tim Drost 13 Feb: 'we see a lot of good things... some genuine interest' and 'you're in the right area of the solution, not missing anything super vital'; positive fit but no decisive lean, no budget, and internal build still alive.

business_case.evidence: Qualitative pain only (onboarding 7 days to 9 months, rising exceptions, COI lapse exposure); Christina 'I don't have a timeline or a budget' and no dollar impact quantified after seven months.

deal_scores_evidence.summary: A genuinely-interested Shortlisted fit deal that has quietly stalled: Christina Behm has pushed the close five times into an opaque internal-approvals loop, the budget owner Tim Drost has not been in a room since the 13 Feb demo, and with a negative AI read and no quantified value case the $120k has nothing concrete to approve.

win_position.text: Sits mid-band on Shortlisted fit: the 13 Feb scenario session validated the exact iSupplier/iContract/iRisk exception workflows Pep runs and Tim Drost confirmed 'you're in the right area, not missing anything super vital,' but it holds well below the 70 Shortlisted cap because power, value case and momentum are all thin.
No quantified value case after seven months: Christina Behm said 'I don't have a timeline or a budget' (17 Dec), so internal approvals have nothing to price the $120k against.

Single-threaded on a mid-level champion into an opaque approvals loop: Christina has pushed the close five times (31 Mar to 28 Jul) and asked to reconnect only the third week of July, on/after the close date.

deal_momentum.text: No buyer-facing deliverable since the 13 Feb demo (143 days); every touch since is rep-initiated chasing or an administrative close-date push.
The last two buyer emails (22 May, 12 Jun) were both 'we're busy, we'll update you' with no new information, so cadence is decaying not building.

customer_commitment.text: Real working investment: Christina sent the NDA, curated the four exception scenarios, and Tim asked buying-stage questions ('how do we buy this... one-time buy of the suite') on 13 Feb.
But commitment is capped: no budget or timeline, the AI/Merlin scope was dropped, and the internal-build option is still explicitly alive.

deal_risk.text: Budget owner Tim Drost has not validated spend or re-engaged since 13 Feb, and the $120k has sat in opaque internal approvals since 26 May.
Scope has narrowed to three core modules with iRisk Lite (a defensive, cost-conscious signal) and the primary alternative is do-nothing inertia plus a possible internal build.

deal_momentum.evidence: No buyer call in 143 days; five close slips; two non-substantive buyer emails.

customer_commitment.evidence: Genuine fit interest but no budget/timeline and AI scope dropped.

deal_risk.evidence: EB unvalidated on budget, negative AIS, scope reduced, do-nothing/internal-build alive, single-thread SPOF.
